titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 3 October 1598

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: I receved two letters from you at Knebworth; to the former I made aunswer from thence by a man of Sir Gerrard Harvies, and now (being called hither by some occasion sooner then I looked for), I wold fain requite your kindnes, and repay you in the same coine yf our market did affoord yt, but the odds runs too much on your side, and the fertilitie of that soyle seemes to flourish with a later springe of pamflets and discourses as ever we are here in the dead of winter.

The erle of Essex is at court in as goode termes (they say) as ever he was, but there be no offices bestowed nor no more shew of bestowing them then the first day the Lord Treasurer died: all men give him theyre voyces to be master of the wardes and yt is thought that serius ocyus yt will light upon him. Here hath ben much speach of new counsaillors and some have ben very neere yt and appointed to be sworne: but the contrarietie of opinions, the number that stand for yt, and the difficultie or rather impossibilitie to please both sides, kepes all backe. Yet yt is certainly thought that Sir John StanhopDuring the illness of the Lord Chamberlain, George Carey, second Lord Hunsdon, a Vice-Chamberlain was needed. Raleigh sought the post, but Stanhope was appointed in February 1601. shalbe shortly vice-chamberlain. Sir Fra: Vere per tot discrimina and with much heave and shove hath got the government of the Brill. One StanlyJohn Stanley declared that he had been employed by the King of Spain to kill Queen Elizabeth. (Cal. S. P. Dom., 1598–1601, pp. 106–107.) that came in sixteen dayes over-land with letters out of Spaine, is lately committed to the Towre. He was very ernest to have private conference with her Majestie pretending matter of great importaunce which he wold by no meanes utter to any body els. He affirmes that the King of Spaine was not dead at his comming away but that he had retired himself and was drawing on. There hath ben some small bickering of late in Ireland wherin there were sevenscore rebells slaine (but not of the maine troupes of Tiron) yet the newes is very welcome and we are glad to make much of a litle. The erle of CumberlandCf. Letter 2. Camden, in his account of the voyage, remarks that he "brought home more victory than booty." (Annales, pp. 497–498. Cf. Purchas, xvi, 29.) is come and some of his shipping; the rest are saide (or rather thought) to be upon the coast, for according to our custome they came scattering, and every man made shift for himself as though they had plaide at formost take up hindmost.

They had goode hap to misse the Spaniards that lay for them with 30 men of warre at the Terceras, and removed from the yland of Flores but the day before the erle came thether. The great expectation of this viage is come to this, that they tooke the towne and castell of Porto-ricco where they found no great riches, for having intelligence of theyre comming the Spaniards had conveyed away theyre cheife wealth. My Lord himself saith that he hath made a saving jorny, but they that understand yt better, say that all he hath brought (wherof the greatest part is sugar and ginger) will not amount to above fifteen or sixteen thowsand pound, which is not halfe the charge of the setting out, besides the adventure and waste of the shipping and the losse of 600 men, what by sword and sicknes. Some finde great fault and say his owne wilfullnes and want of direction overthrew the viage, and that yf he wold have ben advised he might have don them all goode, but he neglected present profit in hope of greater matters, and so forsooke the substaunce for the shadow. I thincke the heat of our sea viages is well alayed by this and the rest, which being no better conducted serve rather to fortefie and confirme, then hinder the ennemie.

I can send you nothing of your private frends for I have neither seen nor heard of any of them. I am here all alone till the terme come that we may be plena curia. The court is at Nonesuch and shold come to Richmond on Tewsday next; yf yt bringe any thinge worth the writing I will not fayle you: and so God kepe you.

postscript.p: To my very goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostend geve these.

div.head: Editor's Note
div.p: Written shortly after Chamberlain's return to London from Knebworth, this letter focuses on two major stories of autumn 1598: the stalled succession struggle following Lord Burghley's death and the deeply disappointing outcome of the Earl of Cumberland's Caribbean voyage. Essex is back at court but getting no offices; Stanhope is expected as Vice-Chamberlain; Vere has finally secured the governorship of the Brill after much difficulty. Most alarming is the arrest of one Stanley, who claims he was sent by Spain to assassinate the Queen—and who insists Philip II is not yet dead. The bulk of the letter, however, concerns Cumberland: his fleet straggled home, the Puerto Rico expedition yielded almost nothing of value, and losses in men and shipping far exceeded any gains. Chamberlain is characteristically sardonic, concluding that England's appetite for sea ventures has surely been cooled by this and similar misadventures.
